title: Number Base Converter (binary, octal, decimal, hex)
shortDescription: Convert numbers between binary, octal, decimal, and hexadecimal. Validates input for the chosen base and shows all four representations.
description: Pick a source base, type a value, and see equivalents in binary, octal, decimal, and hex. Useful for programming, networking, and electronics.
metaDescription: Free number base converter for binary, octal, decimal, and hex. Validates input per base, shows all four representations side by side.

error.invalidForBase: Input contains characters that aren't valid for the selected base.

sections.heading: How positional number bases work
paragraphs: Every base uses positional notation: the rightmost digit is base⁰ = 1, the next is base¹, then base², and so on. In decimal, '255' means 2×100 + 5×10 + 5×1. In binary, '11111111' means 1×128 + 1×64 + ... + 1×1 = 255. The same number, different bases.

Hexadecimal uses 0-9 and A-F to fit 16 values per digit. 'FF' = 15×16 + 15×1 = 255. Octal uses 0-7 (3 bits per digit), and binary uses just 0 and 1. The conversion is mechanical — convert to decimal then back to the target base — and the calculator does it for you for any non-negative integer.

sections.heading: When you'll use each base
paragraphs: Binary (base 2): low-level computer work — bit fields, masks, embedded firmware. Reading register layouts, debugging at the hardware level.
Octal (base 8): Unix file permissions (chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x), some legacy file formats. Less common today.
Decimal (base 10): everything humans count. The default for normal arithmetic.
Hexadecimal (base 16): most common in software — colors (#FF6B35), memory addresses (0x7fff...), MAC addresses, byte representations of binary data, encoded hashes.

sections.heading: Quick reference
paragraphs: 1 byte = 8 bits = 2 hex digits = 256 values (0-255). 1 hex digit = 4 bits = 16 values. 1 octal digit = 3 bits. Hex 'FF' = decimal 255 = binary 11111111. Hex '100' = decimal 256. Hex 'FFFF' = decimal 65535 (max for 16-bit unsigned). Hex 'FFFFFFFF' = decimal 4294967295 (max for 32-bit unsigned).

howTo.name: Pick the source base
howTo.text: Choose binary, octal, decimal, or hex.

howTo.name: Enter your value
howTo.text: Use only digits valid for that base. Hex is case-insensitive.

howTo.name: Read all four bases
howTo.text: Conversion to each of the other three appears alongside.

faq.a: Both 'ff' and 'FF' decode to the same value. We display uppercase by convention.
Yes, prefix with '-'. The calculator displays each base's representation with the sign preserved (e.g., -255 → -FF in hex).
Limited by JavaScript's safe integer range (about 2⁵³). For larger values, use BigInt-aware tools.

faq.q: How do I read 0x prefix or 0b prefix?
faq.a: Strip the prefix before entering. '0xFF' → just 'FF' with hex selected. '0b1010' → '1010' with binary selected.

faq.q: Why does Unix chmod use octal?
faq.a: Permissions have three groups (owner/group/other) of three bits each. Each group's 3 bits map cleanly to one octal digit, so 755 = 111 101 101 = rwx r-x r-x.
Not yet — only non-negative integers are supported. We may add fixed-point support later.

faq.q: What if I see 'invalid for base'?
faq.a: You typed a character that's not allowed in the chosen base. Hex allows 0-9 and A-F; binary only 0 and 1; etc.
